findings.title: Suppression list is never consulted at send time — the tick only checks per-lead status, so a bounced/complained address keeps getting sent to
findings.failure_scenario: A demo/free tenant launches Campaign A and Campaign B both targeting foo@bar.com (two separate lead rows, one per campaign). At launch neither is suppressed, so both schedule sends (campaigns.ts:31-71). The tick sends A-step1 and B-step1 (the `due` query is tenant-wide, not campaign-scoped, tick.ts:42). poll processes A's bounce: reply-processor.ts:35-41 writes a suppression row for foo@bar.com and cancels ONLY A's lead's pending steps (cancelPendingSteps is keyed by lead_id, reply-processor.ts:7-13). B's lead row is a different lead_id and stays global_status='active'. On the next tick, tick.ts:53-58 checks only row.lead_status ('active') and campaign_status — it never reads the suppressions table — so it sends B-step2 to the now hard-bounced foo@bar.com. The only suppression read in the whole codebase is campaigns.ts:34 (launch time). At activation with RealEmailPort this sends real mail to hard-bounced/complained addresses across campaigns and to any suppression created after a campaign launched — deliverability death and a CAN-SPAM/complaint-loop compliance risk. The comment at campaigns.ts:8-12 explicitly (and falsely) claims 'the tick is what actually enforces stop-on-reply/suppression at send time'.
findings.fix: In tick.ts, join suppressions into the `due` query (`LEFT JOIN suppressions sup ON sup.tenant_id = ss.tenant_id AND sup.email = l.email`) and, in the loop, if the row's email is suppressed, mark the send 'skipped' and continue before calling email.send — mirroring the lead_status guard at line 54. Also correct the inaccurate comment at campaigns.ts:8-12.

findings.title: stop_on_reply campaign flag is stored but never read — replies always cancel remaining steps even when the customer set stopOnReply=false
findings.failure_scenario: A customer launches a campaign with stopOnReply:false (a validated, documented option, intents.ts:47; persisted to campaigns.stop_on_reply, campaigns.ts:25). When a reply arrives, processReply() calls cancelPendingSteps() unconditionally (reply-processor.ts:17) — it never loads or checks the campaign's stop_on_reply value (grep confirms stop_on_reply is written at campaigns.ts:25 and read nowhere). So every remaining sequence step is cancelled regardless of the flag. A user who deliberately wants the sequence to continue after a reply (e.g. tolerating auto-responders/OOO replies) silently gets their campaign halted. Demonstrable in the sandbox today via a reply-triggering lead.
findings.fix: In processReply(), look up the campaign's stop_on_reply for ref.campaign_id (the ref already carries campaign_id) and call cancelPendingSteps() only when it is 1. Still record the 'reply' event and set global_status='replied' unconditionally; gate only the step cancellation on the flag.

findings.title: Send window is validated and stored but never enforced — isWithinSendWindow is dead code and the tick sends at any hour
findings.failure_scenario: launch_campaign accepts and stores a sendWindow (intents.ts:44-46, persisted to send_window_json at campaigns.ts:26). scheduler.ts:11 exports isWithinSendWindow to enforce it, but grep confirms it has zero call sites — tick.ts never imports it and never reads send_window_json. So the configured window is inert: at activation, RealEmailPort will send campaign mail at 3am UTC or any hour, ignoring the customer's window, which tanks reply rates and looks spammy. It also violates CLAUDE.md rule (a) 'no unused exports'. Invisible in the sandbox because the demo hardcodes a 0-23 window (demo.ts:63) that makes the check a no-op.
findings.fix: In tick.ts, load each row's campaign send_window_json, parse it, and call isWithinSendWindow(now, window); when the current UTC hour is outside the window, leave the send 'pending' and count it as deferred (like the no-capacity branch at tick.ts:67-70) instead of sending. This wires the existing export in rather than adding new logic.

findings.title: Non-atomic read-check-send-write across the network await lets a concurrent/retried tick double-send, double-decrement caps, and double-count the local ledger
findings.failure_scenario: tick.ts snapshots `due` and reads lead_status at lines 34/54, then awaits email.send at line 80, then writes status='sent'/sent_today+1/'sent' event/ledger at lines 92-127. The dedup guard (status='pending' -> 'sent') is written only AFTER the await. In the sandbox, email.send/poll resolve on microtasks with no real I/O, so the Durable Object input gate never opens and no other event interleaves — tests pass. At activation RealEmailPort.send does a real fetch(); awaiting non-storage I/O opens the DO input gate, so a concurrently-delivered second tick() (B2 alarm-driven, or a retried/duplicated invocation) reads the same row as still 'pending' before the first writes 'sent', and BOTH process it. Consequences: two sends of the same scheduled_send (the email idempotency key `send:tenant:rowid` lets the vendor dedupe the actual message, but the DO state does not dedupe), sent_today incremented twice (corrupting warmup cap accounting, tick.ts:99), a duplicate 'sent' event (inflating metrics, tick.ts:100-112), and — because ledger_entries is inserted with a fresh newId('ledg') and has no idempotency key or unique link to the send (tick.ts:120-127; schema PK is just the UUID, schema.ts:100-107) — the local usage ledger double-counts even though Stripe deduped on `usage:tenant:rowid`. account().usageCents (reporting.ts:80-85) then diverges from actual Stripe charges. This is the 'passes in sandbox, corrupts real data at activation' class the review targets.
findings.fix: Claim each row atomically before sending: run `UPDATE scheduled_sends SET status='sending' WHERE id=? AND status='pending'` and check meta.rowsWritten===1; if 0, another tick already claimed it — skip. Only then call email.send and finalize to 'sent'. Give ledger_entries an idempotency anchor: add a source_send_id column with a UNIQUE constraint and use INSERT OR IGNORE keyed on row.id so a reprocessed send can never write a second usage entry. This makes the DO ledger match the idempotent vendor charge.

findings.title: Send side-effects are committed before billing.recordUsage, and a recordUsage failure loses the usage and aborts the whole tick batch
findings.failure_scenario: Per send, tick.ts writes status='sent', increments sent_today, and inserts the 'sent' event (lines 92-112, all synchronous/committed) BEFORE awaiting billing.recordUsage (line 114) and inserting the ledger entry (line 120). recordUsage can throw at activation (RealBillingPort.recordUsage currently throws NotActivatedError unconditionally, real/billing-port.ts:10-17; a live Stripe call can throw on network/4xx). Because the throw is unhandled, it propagates out of runTick, aborting the loop: the current send is left marked 'sent' with the mailbox cap consumed and a 'sent' event recorded, but NO Stripe usage and NO ledger entry — the customer was sent to but never billed (revenue leak) and the local ledger/account usageCents under-reports — and every remaining due row in the batch is left unprocessed. Usage is silently lost.
findings.fix: Record usage as part of the same claim/commit unit as marking the send sent, and wrap the per-row billing in try/catch so a single recordUsage failure marks that row for retry (or rolls its state back to 'pending') instead of throwing out of the batch. Order the writes so that either both the 'sent' state and the usage record land or neither does — do not commit 'sent'+cap consumption before usage is durably recorded.
